title: Unidirectional / One-Way Link Failure
description: Link appears up locally but only one direction is passing traffic — classic split-brain or unidirectional failure
root_cause_analysis: A one-way (unidirectional) link exists when the local interface shows oper_status=up but the remote peer cannot receive frames (or vice versa). This is particularly dangerous as routing protocols may not detect the failure immediately, leading to black-holing of traffic. Caused by a failed Tx or Rx path in one direction (e.g. one fibre strand broken, one SFP laser dead, Rx only failed), or UDLD/BFD timeout.

symptoms: Local oper_status is up, remote oper_status is also up
Traffic flows in one direction only
UDLD neighbour messages not received from peer
BFD session down while interface remains up
Routing protocol adjacency loss (OSPF/BGP) without physical link alarm
High in_discards or zero counters on one side

title: Interface Flapping with Concurrent High Error Rate
description: Interface oscillates between up/down states AND exhibits elevated CRC/input/output errors — combined physical and logical failure
root_cause_analysis: Unlike a simple link flap (negotiation or loose cable), flap-with-errors indicates physical-layer degradation that is simultaneously causing signal instability and data corruption. The interface may briefly come up during each cycle but corrupts frames before dropping again. Typical causes: severely degraded optical signal near sensitivity threshold, failing SFP with intermittent Tx, or a partially damaged fibre.

title: Elevated Interface Error Rate - CRC / Input Errors
description: High CRC or input errors on an otherwise stable, operationally-up interface — caused by physical-layer corruption
root_cause_analysis: High rate of CRC or input errors on an otherwise-up interface indicates physical-layer corruption at the cable, connector, or transceiver level. The interface is not flapping — this is pure receive-side frame corruption where frames arrive but fail integrity checks. Unlike output queue drops (see link.congestion_drop), the problem is not traffic volume but signal quality: a bad cable, dirty connector, failing optic, or duplex mismatch causes the receiving end to discard frames as uncorrupted delivery is impossible. MTU mismatch causes a related symptom — oversized frames dropped as giants.

No output queue depth increase — this is receive-side corruption, not transmit-side congestion

title: Interface Output Queue Drops - Congestion-Induced Packet Loss
description: Output queue drops (out_discards / tail drops) on an operationally-up interface caused by traffic volume exceeding link capacity
root_cause_analysis: Output queue drops occur when traffic being sent out of an interface consistently or momentarily exceeds the physical link capacity. Unlike CRC or input errors (see link.high_errors), the frames are correctly formed — the problem is rate: the output queue fills faster than the link can drain it, and tail-drop or WRED discards begin. This causes silent packet loss — the link shows no physical errors, no flapping, and the device remains fully manageable. Distinguish between sustained oversubscription (chronic — link needs upgrading or traffic re-engineering) and microbursts (transient — QoS tuning or queue-depth increase may help). CPU-induced drops (see device.cpu_overload) present similarly but are driven by control-plane saturation, not link capacity.

QoS queue depth at maximum — tail-drop active
Traffic utilisation on interface near or at 100%

title: Device Not Reachable - Complete Management Loss
description: Device is unreachable from the management system — all polling methods (SNMP, ICMP, gRPC) have failed
root_cause_analysis: Device is not responding to any management queries. title: Device Power Failure - PSU or Feed Loss
description: One or more power supply units have failed, or the primary power feed has been lost
root_cause_analysis: Power events are among the highest-impact failures as they affect all services on the device simultaneously. A single-PSU failure on a dual-PSU device may not cause an outage but leaves no redundancy. A dual-PSU loss or main feed failure causes total device outage. Correlated with UPS events, circuit breaker trips, or datacenter power alarms. Physical site visit is often required to confirm.

title: Device CPU Overload - Control Plane Saturated
description: Device CPU is critically high, starving management and control-plane processes of cycles
root_cause_analysis: Control-plane CPU saturation causes management processes (SNMP agent, SSH, syslog) to become unresponsive, while data-plane forwarding (hardware-based) may continue. This creates a misleading state where traffic appears to flow but the device cannot be managed or queried. Causes include: routing protocol instability flooding the CPU with LSAs/updates, a traffic-punt attack, runaway software process, or memory pressure causing paging.

title: Device Unexpected Reboot / Crash-Reload
description: Device reloaded unexpectedly — no planned maintenance window associated with the event
root_cause_analysis: An unplanned reboot causes a brief total outage during reload (typically 3–15 minutes for most platforms). The device may come back fully functional, or it may reload into a degraded state (boot loop, partial supervisor, rommon). Root cause must be established from the reload reason and crash info before the next occurrence. Common causes: software panic/kernel crash, hardware fault (memory ECC error, supervisor crash), watchdog expiry, or in rare cases an accidental 'reload' command.

title: LAG / Port-Channel Completely Down - All Members Lost
description: Link Aggregation Group (LAG/LACP/port-channel/bundle) is fully down — no active member interfaces remain
root_cause_analysis: A LAG going completely down means every member link has failed OR the LACP control protocol has determined the bundle is unusable (min-links threshold breached, actor/partner system-ID mismatch, or PDU timeout). This is a service-affecting event because the logical interface carries aggregated traffic from all members. Critical distinction from link.down: the physical member ports may still show oper_status=up while the LAG itself is down — e.g., all members up but LACP negotiation failed. Always inspect the LAG-level state first, then drill into member-level state and LACP PDU exchange logs.

title: LAG Degraded - One or More Members Lost, Reduced Bandwidth
description: LAG/port-channel is operationally up but has fewer active members than configured — bandwidth is reduced and redundancy is compromised
root_cause_analysis: A degraded LAG is among the most dangerous failure modes in enterprise and carrier networks because it remains functionally operational — traffic continues to flow — while silently operating below intended capacity. Monitoring systems often show the LAG as 'green' while its bandwidth has halved or worse. This condition can persist undetected until the remaining members also fail, causing a sudden full outage. The root cause is typically a single physical member failure, an LACP PDU loss on one member, or a misconfiguration applied to a subset of members. Must be distinguished from link.lag_down (all members lost) and link.down (individual physical interface failure not part of a LAG).

title: Spanning Tree Loop - Layer 2 Broadcast Storm
description: A Spanning Tree Protocol (STP) loop has formed, causing a broadcast storm that is consuming all available bandwidth and CPU on affected switches
root_cause_analysis: An STP loop occurs when a port that should be in STP blocking state erroneously transitions to forwarding, creating a cycle in the L2 topology. Broadcast, multicast, and unknown-unicast frames loop indefinitely, doubling every round-trip until bandwidth is exhausted and switch CPUs spike to 100%. Unlike almost all other link failures, the physical interfaces remain operationally up — the loop is a control-plane and forwarding-plane failure. This is one of the most severe and rapidly escalating L2 faults: a full broadcast storm can saturate a 10G link within seconds. Detection relies on correlated spikes across multiple devices simultaneously, not a single interface going down. Root causes include: BPDU Guard not configured on access ports, an unmanaged switch inserted by a user, STP root election upset, or a PortFast misconfiguration on a trunk port.

Syslog may show 'topology change' notifications or MAC flap warnings
Storm-control or rate-limiter triggers on affected ports
Physical interfaces remain up — no LOS alarm

title: Linecard / FPC Hardware Failure - Partial Device Outage
description: A linecard, FPC (Flexible PIC Concentrator), or line module has faulted or been taken offline — all interfaces on that card are lost while the device chassis and supervisor remain reachable
root_cause_analysis: Linecard failures are a distinct class from full device outages: the supervisor, management plane, and all ports on healthy cards remain fully operational. Only the ports physically resident on the failed card go dark. This makes the fault appear as a simultaneous multi-interface link-down event correlated to a single slot, rather than a device-level outage. Causes range from ASIC hardware faults and ECC memory errors in the forwarding engine to power budget exhaustion in the chassis (not enough wattage to power the card), overheating, or a firmware panic on the FPC. On Juniper MX/PTX platforms this surfaces as FPC offline/restart events; on Cisco ASR/Catalyst as linecard 'faulty' or LC offline; on Nokia SR as MDA/MDA failure. Critical distinction from device.unreachable: management is alive, only a slot's worth of interfaces are affected.

title: Supervisor / Route Engine Failover - HA Switchover Event
description: The active supervisor, Route Engine (RE), or RSP has failed over to the standby — control-plane traffic interrupted briefly; data-plane may be preserved via NSF/NSR/GRES
root_cause_analysis: Modern carrier-grade and enterprise core devices run dual supervisors/Route Engines for high availability. When the active supervisor fails or is administratively switched over, the standby takes control. This produces a signature that NMS interprets as 'device down then up' — but it is fundamentally different from a true outage. With NSF (Non-Stop Forwarding) or GRES (Graceful Routing Engine Switchover), the data-plane forwarding table survives and traffic continues; routing protocol adjacencies may briefly blip. Without NSF/GRES, all adjacencies reset and convergence time applies. Critical to distinguish from device.unexpected_reboot (single supervisor crash/reload) and device.unreachable (no recovery). Always check whether the event was planned (maintenance window) before escalating, and determine whether data-plane traffic was preserved.

title: Device Boot Loop - Stuck in Repeated Restart Cycle
description: Device is caught in a continuous boot loop — it becomes briefly reachable during boot then crashes and restarts, never reaching a stable operating state
root_cause_analysis: A boot loop is one of the most severe device failure modes because the device is stuck in a state where it cannot self-recover. It appears to NMS as a series of rapid device.unreachable / available cycles, with uptime never exceeding a few minutes. The device may be stuck in rommon (ROM Monitor mode), repeatedly crashing during OS initialization, or failing POST (Power-On Self Test). Unlike device.unexpected_reboot (a one-time crash that recovers normally), a boot loop is a persistent, cyclic condition that requires manual console access to resolve. Causes include: a corrupt or incompatible boot image following a failed upgrade, persistent OS software bugs triggered at startup, hardware DIMM failures that prevent memory initialization, or a broken file system on the boot media. Time-to-resolution is high — always requires hands-on or console access, and often a field dispatch.

title: Device Memory Exhaustion - Control Plane RAM Depletion
description: Device RAM is critically depleted — routing processes are being killed or starved, the routing table may be truncated, and the device is at risk of a crash or partial forwarding failure
root_cause_analysis: Memory exhaustion on a network device is a slow-burn failure that can go undetected until it causes sudden service impact. Unlike CPU overload (which is fast and obvious), memory depletion progresses gradually as routing tables grow, software memory leaks accumulate, or a new feature consumes more memory than anticipated. When the OS runs out of free memory, it begins OOM-killing processes — typically starting with non-critical ones, then escalating to routing processes (bgpd, rpd, ospfd). The result is routing table truncation, BGP session drops due to memory allocation failures for updates, or a full control-plane crash. Critical distinction from device.cpu_overload: CPU utilization may look entirely normal while memory is critically low. Always check memory metrics independently, not as a proxy for device health.

title: Interface Errdisable - Automated OS Protection State
description: Interface placed in errdisable state by the OS due to a security or protocol violation — operationally distinct from manual administrative shutdown
root_cause_analysis: Errdisable is an automated, cause-specific protective mechanism where the OS takes a port offline in response to a detected violation. Unlike link.admin_down (operator-initiated with admin_status=down), errdisable keeps admin_status=up while setting oper_status=down and logs the specific errdisable cause. Each cause requires a completely different investigation and recovery path — BPDU guard violation requires inspecting what device was connected; port-security requires reviewing MAC address bindings; storm-control requires investigating broadcast or multicast sources. The interface will NOT return to service on its own unless errdisable recovery timers are configured for that specific cause.

title: Routing Protocol Adjacency Loss on Up Link
description: BGP, OSPF, or IS-IS routing adjacency has dropped while the physical interface remains operationally up — a pure control-plane failure with no physical alarm
root_cause_analysis: Routing adjacency loss on a physically up link is one of the most disruptive and hardest-to-detect failure modes: the interface counters look healthy, the link shows up/up, but the routing protocol has lost its neighbour and traffic is being black-holed or rerouted. This class of fault does not generate any physical alarm and is detected only by monitoring routing protocol state directly. Common causes include: hold-timer expiry due to CPU overload on one peer preventing hello or keepalive transmission, MTU mismatch preventing OSPF DBD exchange from completing, routing authentication key mismatch after a config change, or a route policy change that filters all prefixes causing BGP to receive no updates and eventually tear down the session.

title: TCAM Exhaustion - Hardware Forwarding Table Full
description: Hardware TCAM is critically full — new routes, ACL entries, or MAC/ARP entries cannot be programmed, causing silent packet drops with the device fully reachable and all interfaces up
root_cause_analysis: TCAM exhaustion is one of the most operationally dangerous failure modes because the device remains fully reachable, all interfaces are up, and the control-plane RIB still holds all routes — yet traffic is silently dropped because the hardware forwarding table has no space to program new entries. Unlike system RAM exhaustion, TCAM is a fixed-capacity hardware resource used for line-rate lookups that cannot be expanded with software. When TCAM fills, the OS silently fails to install new routes or ACL entries in hardware. Operators see traffic loss on specific destinations with no alarm because the device is healthy from a management perspective. TCAM banks on most platforms are shared between multiple features — IPv4 routes, IPv6 routes, ACLs, QoS policies, MAC and ARP tables — which compete for the same fixed resource pool.

title: Management Plane Lockout - AAA or ACL Access Blocked
description: Device management access is blocked due to AAA server failure, management ACL misconfiguration, or management VRF routing loss — device is operationally healthy and forwarding traffic normally
root_cause_analysis: Management plane lockout is a configuration or control-plane access failure where the device is physically healthy, all data-plane interfaces are up, and traffic is flowing normally — but the NMS cannot authenticate or route to the management IP. This is fundamentally different from device.unreachable where the device itself has failed. The three most common causes are: TACACS or RADIUS server unreachable causing all SSH and SNMP authentication to fail; a management ACL or firewall rule change that inadvertently blocks the NMS source IP; or loss of the route to the management IP in the management VRF. In all cases the device continues to forward data-plane traffic without interruption. Misdiagnosis as device.unreachable results in unnecessary field dispatch — the device can be recovered via console without physical intervention.

symptoms: NMS reports device as unreachable — availability = 0
Data-plane traffic through the device is unaffected — customer services healthy
SSH connection to management IP is refused or times out
SNMP polling times out with no response from SNMP agent
TACACS or RADIUS server may be showing connectivity issues in parallel
Console access to device succeeds — device is operationally healthy
Device syslog shows AAA authentication failures or No AAA server available
negative_indicators: Console access also fails — device is truly down, see device.unreachable or device.power_failure
Data-plane traffic is also affected — not a management-only lockout
sysUpTime has reset — device rebooted, see device.unexpected_reboot
All interfaces operationally down — hardware failure, see device.unreachable
psu_status failed — power cause, see device.power_failure
ha_state changed — HA event, see device.supervisor_failover

title: BGP Session Down - Routing Protocol Peering Loss
description: One or more BGP sessions on the device have dropped, causing loss of routing information from those peers and potential traffic black-holing for affected prefixes
root_cause_analysis: BGP session loss is a high-impact routing event because BGP carries inter-domain routes, customer VPN prefixes, and in many architectures the full Internet routing table. Unlike link.routing_adjacency_down which focuses on a single interface adjacency, this entry covers device-wide BGP session failures that may affect multiple peers simultaneously or indicate a systemic issue on the device itself. A BGP session going to Idle or Active means the TCP session underlying it has either been reset by the peer, timed out due to missing keepalives, or refused due to authentication or policy failure. The impact depends on whether alternative paths exist: in a redundant topology, prefix withdrawal triggers reconvergence; in a single-path topology, traffic is black-holed immediately.

symptoms: BGP session state is Idle, Active, or Connect for one or more peers
bgp_session_availability_pct has dropped below 100 — fewer established sessions than configured
Routing table is missing prefixes that were previously received from the affected peer
BGP NOTIFICATION message logged — peer sent an explicit session teardown with error code
Device is reachable via management — availability = 100
Traffic loss on prefixes sourced from the affected BGP peer
BFD session down for a BGP-tracked peer if BFD is used for fast BGP failover
negative_indicators: Physical link is also down — adjacency loss is a consequence of physical failure, see link.down
All routing protocols affected simultaneously — device-level issue, see device.cpu_overload or device.memory_exhaustion
Device is unreachable — BGP drop is a consequence, see device.unreachable first
oper_status down on the BGP peering interface — physical failure, see link.down
cpu_utilization_percent > 90 — CPU may be dropping BGP keepalives, see device.cpu_overload
memory_utilization_percent > 90 — OOM may have killed bgpd, see device.memory_exhaustion
All BGP sessions dropped simultaneously at reboot time — systemic crash, check device.unexpected_reboot

hypotheses.description: BGP hold timer expired — the remote peer stopped sending keepalives. Most commonly caused by CPU overload on the remote device, transit network congestion dropping BGP keepalives, or QoS misconfiguration deprioritising BGP traffic
log_patterns: BGP: .* neighbor .* Down - Hold Timer Expired

hypotheses.description: Underlying TCP session was reset by the peer or an intermediary — firewall state table expiry, NAT timeout, or a network device in the path reset the TCP connection
log_patterns: BGP: .* neighbor .* Down - Peer closing connection

hypotheses.description: BGP MD5 or TCP-AO authentication key mismatch — a configuration change rotated the password on one side without updating the peer, causing all BGP OPEN messages to be rejected

hypotheses.description: The BGP process restarted due to OOM kill, software bug, or watchdog — all sessions reset simultaneously and are reconverging

title: Copper Link Auto-Negotiation Failure - Speed/Duplex Mismatch
description: Copper 10/100/1000BASE-T interface fails to establish or holds at incorrect speed/duplex due to auto-negotiation failure
root_cause_analysis: On copper Ethernet interfaces, auto-negotiation (IEEE 802.3u) exchanges speed and duplex capability via Fast Link Pulses. When one side has auto-neg disabled and the other has it enabled, the auto-neg side falls back to half-duplex, causing late collisions and high error rates. A forced speed/duplex mismatch causes the same. Unlike optical failures there is no LOS — the link may appear up while performing poorly. Symptom profile: high late-collision counters, input errors, duplex mismatch syslog, or the interface cycling through negotiation states.

symptoms: Late collisions or input errors rising on copper interface
Interface shows duplex mismatch in show interface output
Interface stuck in half-duplex when full-duplex expected
Interface flapping during negotiation phase
Throughput severely degraded (often 50–80% below expected)
oper_status may be up but error counters escalating rapidly
negative_indicators: Optical interface (SFP/XFP/QSFP) — auto-neg failure applies to copper only, see link.down or link.degraded
No error counters present and link is stable — this is not a negotiation failure
LOS alarm present — optical failure, see link.down
flap_count > 3 with CRC errors — degraded physical layer, see link.flap_with_errors
oper_status is down with LOS — physical cable/optic failure, see link.down
admin_status is down — intentional shutdown, see link.admin_down
BFD/UDLD down on otherwise stable link — unidirectional, see link.one_way
lag_active_members reduced — bundle member issue, see link.lag_degraded
log_patterns: %CDP-4-DUPLEX_MISMATCH: duplex mismatch discovered on .* (not full duplex)

PHY: copper link .* settled at half-duplex due to mismatched capability
%LINK-3-UPDOWN: Interface .*, changed state to down (negotiation timeout)
EtherChannel: member .* failed negotiation — speed mismatch

hypotheses.description: One side has auto-negotiation disabled (forced speed/duplex) while the other side uses auto-neg — the auto-neg side falls back to half-duplex
Both sides have incompatible forced speed or duplex settings, preventing link establishment or causing performance degradation
Cable category too low (e.g. Cat3 on GigE), excessive length, damaged pairs, or PoE pair interference preventing reliable negotiation
Interface negotiated to half-duplex as a fallback, creating a collision domain that saturates as utilisation increases

title: MPLS LSP Down - TE-Tunnel or LDP Path Failure
description: MPLS Label Switched Path (LDP FEC or RSVP-TE tunnel) is down while the underlying physical interface may remain up
root_cause_analysis: MPLS forwarding depends on label distribution (LDP or RSVP-TE) maintaining an end-to-end label binding across all transit hops. The physical links may be fully operational while an LSP is down because: the LDP neighbour session dropped, an RSVP-TE path could not be signalled or rerouted after a topology change, a configuration change removed MPLS from an interface, or a transit link that carried the LSP failed and no alternative path was found within the configured retry window. Traffic relying on this LSP is black-holed or falls back to IP forwarding (if LDP) or is dropped (if traffic-engineered strict path with no fallback).

symptoms: MPLS tunnel or LDP FEC shows operational state down
mpls_lsp_state or mpls_tunnel_state is down
LDP neighbour session lost in syslog
RSVP-TE path error or RESV teardown received
Traffic over affected LSP black-holed or rerouted
Downstream VPN (L3VPN/VPLS/EVPN) service impacted
Physical interfaces on both endpoints remain up

BGP session to PE is down — control-plane issue, see device.bgp_session_down
Device fully unreachable — see device.unreachable

LDP session is up and FEC is resolved — this is not an LSP-down condition
Only one VPN customer affected while other VPNs work — VRF/route-target misconfiguration, not link.mpls_lsp_down
oper_status is down on physical member — check link.down on transit hop first

title: VLAN Trunk Misconfiguration - 802.1Q Trunk or VLAN Mismatch
description: 802.1Q trunk is up but specific VLANs are not passing traffic due to allowed-VLAN mismatch, native VLAN conflict, or port-mode inconsistency
root_cause_analysis: The physical link and 802.1Q trunk are operationally up, but one or more VLANs are silently dropping traffic. This occurs when the allowed VLAN list is inconsistent between the two trunk endpoints, when the native VLAN (untagged) differs between peers causing traffic to land in the wrong VLAN, or when one side is in access mode while the other is in trunk mode. VTP domain mismatch or VLAN pruning can also remove VLANs from the active list without physical-layer symptoms. This is a configuration-plane fault — link counters may show no errors.

Asymmetric reachability — one direction may work

oper_status is down — physical issue, not VLAN misconfiguration
admin_status is down — intentional shutdown, see link.admin_down

Only a single device unreachable — end-host configuration issue, not trunk misconfiguration

title: Routing Table Corruption - FIB Loss or Routing Process Restart
description: Routing process crash or FIB programming failure causing routes to disappear from the forwarding table while the device remains manageable
root_cause_analysis: The management plane is reachable (SNMP, SSH respond) but data-plane forwarding is broken because the FIB (Forwarding Information Base) is empty, stale, or incomplete. This happens when a routing daemon (ospfd, bgpd, isis) crashes and restarts with a clean table before re-convergence, when the ASIC FIB programming fails due to TCAM exhaustion or hardware error, or when a route redistribution policy change inadvertently withdraws a large prefix block. The device appears healthy from a management perspective but is silently dropping or misforwarding traffic.

title: Device High Temperature - Pre-Shutdown Thermal Warning
description: Device temperature sensors are reporting critical or near-critical readings — protective shutdown is imminent if not resolved
root_cause_analysis: Network devices monitor internal temperature via multiple sensors (inlet, outlet, ASIC, PSU). When temperature exceeds a minor threshold, a warning alarm is raised. If temperature continues to rise past a major threshold, the device initiates a protective shutdown to prevent hardware damage. Root causes split between device-internal (fan failure, partially blocked airflow) and facility-level (datacenter cooling failure, hot aisle/cold aisle containment breach). Unlike device.power_failure where the device is already down, this RCA fires while the device is still operational — action window is available.

Device logs show temperature warning syslog messages

Temperature is within normal range — this is not a thermal event

Temperature is nominal but device is unreachable — see device.unreachable

title: Device Storage Full - Flash / SSD Capacity Exhausted
description: Device internal flash or SSD storage is at or near capacity, preventing log writes, config saves, software updates, and core dump collection
root_cause_analysis: Network device storage (compact flash, USB flash, internal SSD) is consumed by several competing processes: OS software images, running and startup configuration, syslog files, crash-info/core dumps, and SNMP MIB data. When storage reaches 100%, the device cannot write new log entries (causing silent loss of diagnostic data), cannot save configuration changes (a risk for config rollback), and cannot accept software upgrades. On some platforms a full disk also prevents the OS from writing temporary files required for process operation, causing process crashes. Regular housekeeping is required but is often neglected on long-running devices.

title: NTP Desynchronisation - Clock Drift Causing Auth / Certificate Failures
description: Device NTP clock has drifted significantly from reference time, causing authentication failures, certificate validation errors, and unreliable log timestamps
root_cause_analysis: Network device time synchronisation via NTP is foundational to security protocols (Kerberos, RADIUS, TACACS+ timestamp validation, TLS certificate validity windows) and to log correlation across the NOC. When NTP desynchronises — because all configured NTP servers become unreachable, because an NTP MD5/SHA key is rotated on the server without updating the client, or because a sudden large clock jump exceeds NTP's step threshold — the device starts rejecting valid certificates as expired, may fail AAA authentication due to timestamp skew beyond the tolerance window (typically ±300 seconds), and produces logs with incorrect timestamps that corrupt RCA timeline reconstruction.

symptoms: NTP status shows unsynchronised or stratum 16 (unsync)
Authentication failures (RADIUS/TACACS+) with timestamp-related errors
TLS/SSL certificate errors — valid certificate reported as expired
Log timestamps diverging from wall-clock time
ntp_offset_ms above acceptable threshold (typically >1000ms)
Device may reject SSH keys or SNMP v3 USM due to time window check
negative_indicators: NTP is synchronised and offset is within tolerance — this is not NTP desync
AAA server is unreachable (network path issue) — see device.management_plane_lockout
Device is fully unreachable — time desync alone does not cause full outage, see device.unreachable
Certificate is genuinely expired (not due to clock skew) — certificate lifecycle issue, not NTP
Only syslog timestamps wrong but authentication works — cosmetic only, low severity
